region_of_origin: Mediterranean, Middle East, and North America

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pine nuts is approximately 28g (one small handful), providing around 196 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Pine nuts, dried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 12147
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pine nuts, dried (raw, unroasted) used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are consistent with USDA data and are preserved from the input record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pine nuts are not individually listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on the published GI range for mixed nuts and seeds (GI 14–20) from Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 4.5 × 0.28) / 100 = 0.19, rounded to 0.3 per 28g serving. The very low carbohydrate content (4.5g/100g) ensures negligible glycaemic impact regardless of GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3mg/100g) and very high potassium (600mg/100g) yield an exceptional sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Raw pine nuts are highly suitable for hypertension management; consume 20–30g per day as a snack, strictly avoiding salted or flavoured commercial preparations that substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (4.5g/100g), low sugar (3.4g/100g), high dietary fibre (5.1g/100g), and a low estimated GI (~15) result in negligible postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pine nuts are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a 20–30g portion provides minimal glycaemic load while the high MUFA/PUFA content and fibre support insulin sensitivity — avoid sweetened or candied pine nut preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fatty acid profile with high MUFA (18.8g/100g) and PUFA (34.1g/100g), low saturated fat (4.9g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high vitamin E (9.33mg/100g) supporting endothelial and lipid health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of a small handful (28g) of raw pine nuts is consistent with cardioprotective dietary patterns; the high MUFA/PUFA ratio supports LDL reduction and the high magnesium (251mg/100g) and potassium (600mg/100g) content further benefit vascular function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA/PUFA content (52.9g/100g combined), vitamin E (9.33mg/100g), magnesium (251mg/100g), and dietary fibre (5.1g/100g) collectively support anti-inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pine nuts are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; choose raw or dry-roasted unsalted varieties and introduce gradually if new to the diet given the possibility of pine nut allergy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pine nuts (Pinus spp.) are the edible seeds of pine trees, harvested from pine cones and consumed raw or lightly toasted. Raw pine nuts are cream to ivory in colour, teardrop-shaped, with a firm yet tender texture and a delicate, mildly sweet, buttery flavour. They are a nutrient-dense tree nut providing 699 kcal per 100g, with fat as the dominant macronutrient (70g/100g), predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated (18.8g) and polyunsaturated (34.1g) fatty acids, with low saturated fat (4.9g) and zero trans fat. Protein content is moderate at 13g/100g, carbohydrates are very low at 4.5g/100g, and dietary fibre is high at 5.1g/100g. Pine nuts are exceptionally rich in vitamin E (9.33mg/100g), magnesium (251mg/100g), phosphorus (575mg/100g), zinc (6.45mg/100g), and potassium (600mg/100g), while sodium is negligible at 3mg/100g. Their estimated glycaemic index is approximately 15 (low), making them suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, pine nuts are well-suited for individuals managing hypertension (excellent sodium-to-potassium ratio), Type 2 Diabetes (very low glycaemic load), and cardiovascular disease (cardioprotective fatty acid profile). They are used globally in Mediterranean cuisine (pesto, salads, pastries), Middle Eastern cooking, and as a standalone snack. Pine nuts are vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher, but are classified as tree nuts and represent a known allergen.
common_user_queries: Are pine nuts good for diabetes?
Are pine nuts high in fat?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for pine nuts, dried (NDB 12147). Fatty acid breakdown, micronutrients, and vitamin data sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct pine nut GI entry exists in the University of Sydney database.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central; pine nuts are a plant food and inherently contain no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is 0.0mcg/100g — naturally zero as pine nuts are a plant-based food. Vitamin D is 0.0mcg/100g — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; pine nuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. 2. Saturated fat value of 4.9g/100g, monounsaturated fat 18.8g/100g, polyunsaturated fat 34.1g/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 12147) as HPB input record did not include fatty acid breakdown. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on published GI range for mixed nuts and seeds (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283); no direct pine nut GI entry found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. No mandatory multilingual names required: pine nuts are a globally traded raw ingredient with no single national cuisine origin; G6 multilingual gate is not triggered. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC and FoodOn URI FOODON_03301446 are defensible matches for pine nuts as a whole nut commodity. SNOMED CT code 412061001 is the substance-level entry for pine nut.

improvement_actions_taken: Added saturated_fat_g (4.9g), monounsaturated_fat_g (18.8g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (34.1g), trans_fat_g (0.0g) from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy to supplement HPB input.
Added full micronutrient profile (vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, C, A, D, E, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) from USDA SR Legacy.

Expanded aliases to 12 entries covering English variants, romanized Italian forms, and regional names.
